note: Category 9. Investigated 2026-07-03 by a dedicated read-only agent AFTER the main workflow (safety+build investigators had failed on the session limit). Self-verified by the agent; THEN three findings were independently checked by the lead auditor (see _lead_verification) and two severities were corrected.

_lead_verification.checked: 2026-07-03 by lead auditor, read-only pio/grep, no hardware.
_lead_verification.ledc_api: CONFIRMED Esp32LedcPwm.cpp:10-17 uses ledcSetup(channel,freq,res) + ledcAttachPin(pin,channel) + ledcWrite(channel,duty) — the core-2.x channel API (removed/replaced by ledcAttach(pin,freq,res)+ledcWrite(pin,duty) in Arduino-ESP32 core 3.x).
_lead_verification.resolved_versions: CORRECTION: `pio pkg list -e esp32dev` shows BOTH repos currently resolve to Platform espressif32 @ 7.0.1 -> framework-arduinoespressif32 @ 3.20017 (= Arduino core 2.0.17). So (a) the unpinned control-fw TODAY resolves to the SAME 7.0.1 as soundlight's pin and builds fine; a fresh resolve does NOT currently pull core 3.x. (b) The two boards are NOT currently on divergent cores.
_lead_verification.severity_corrections: Finding 'unpinned+LEDC' downgraded critical->high (latent reproducibility risk, not a live build failure — but pinning is still worth doing NOW as it is trivial). Finding 'two boards divergent' downgraded high->medium (latent divergence; currently identical).
_lead_verification.serialport_rebuild: CONFIRMED: package.json scripts = setup/start/demo/test/test:watch/build/fetch-mediamtx — NO 'app:rebuild' script; build = 'electron-builder --win' with no rebuild; @electron/rebuild ^3.6.0 IS a devDependency but nothing invokes it; electron-builder.yml:34 references a nonexistent 'app:rebuild step'; no beforeBuild/afterPack hook. Finding stands at high.

summary: The native-test-first, HAL-seam architecture is genuinely well built and the docs are unusually thorough for a hobby project, but the build/deploy layer has a latent reproducibility hazard and several deploy gaps a green CI hides. Biggest issue: control-fw pins nothing (platform = espressif32) yet its LEDC HAL uses the channel API (ledcSetup/ledcAttachPin/ledcWrite(channel,...)) removed in Arduino-ESP32 core 3.x; today the unpinned platform still resolves to 7.0.1 / core 2.0.17 (so it builds and matches soundlight), but a future platform release shipping core 3.x would silently break the gift firmware. soundlight-fw is correctly pinned (@ ~7.0.1) precisely because it needs the legacy IDF-4.4 I2S driver. On the ground-station side, CI proves only that pure logic tests pass; it never packages the Electron app nor rebuilds serialport for Electron's ABI, and electron-builder.yml references an 'app:rebuild' step that does not exist — so the shippable .exe will likely fail to load serialport and silently run telemetry-disabled. board=esp32dev is correct for a WROOM-32 DevKit. Reproducibility is the core theme: it builds today largely because the local caches happen to hold 7.0.1.
well_designed: control-fw platformio.ini documents the non-obvious ${env:esp32dev.build_flags} interpolation with an explicit comment that a child build_flags REPLACES the parent's rather than appending (lines 20-22).

native test env is hardened three ways against pulling in hardware code: test_build_src=no, explicit lib_ignore of every *_hal_esp32 lib, and HAL library.json declaring platforms:espressif32 so the LDF won't select them on native — commented as belt-and-suspenders.

soundlight-fw platform is pinned to espressif32 @ ~7.0.1 with an in-file comment tying the pin to the concrete dependency (legacy IDF 4.4 driver/i2s.h) — the right instinct; its absence in control-fw is the glaring asymmetry.

Gift-firmware vs bench-firmware is a deliberate, documented compile-time split: tuning console + sim feeder gated behind -DW17_TUNING_CONSOLE / -DW17_SIM_CRSF_FEEDER, so the delivered esp32dev build has no UART0 console surface; D8 Phase 11 says to reflash plain esp32dev before gifting.

scripts/run.js and scripts/ensure-electron.js solve two real Electron dev-env failures (VS Code leaking ELECTRON_RUN_AS_NODE; script-gated Electron postinstall) cross-platform, with the why explained in code + README.
package-lock.json IS committed for the ground station; mediamtx (v1.9.3) and NeoPixel (^1.12.0) are pinned/commented, so the JS/asset surface is largely reproducible.
Viewer-only ground-station design means a build/deploy failure there can never affect the car; elrs-joystick-control + VLC zero-code fallback documented as always-available.
D8_BENCH_BRINGUP.md is a strong, safety-ordered flashing/bring-up runbook: which env on which board, wheels-off gates, failsafe-proof-before-ESC-power.

findings.title: control-fw platform is UNPINNED and its LEDC HAL uses the core-2.x-only channel API removed in core 3.x — latent build-reproducibility hazard for the gift firmware
findings.evidence: platformio.ini: 'platform = espressif32' with no @version. Esp32LedcPwm.cpp uses ledcSetup(channel_,kFrequencyHz,kResolutionBits); ledcAttachPin(pin_,channel_); ledcWrite(channel_,duty) — the channel-oriented LEDC API replaced in Arduino-ESP32 core 3.x by ledcAttach(pin,freq,res)/ledcWrite(pin,duty). soundlight-fw pins @ ~7.0.1 for exactly this class of reason; control-fw does not.
findings.lead_verification: LEDC channel API usage CONFIRMED. BUT verified the unpinned platform currently resolves to espressif32 @ 7.0.1 -> arduino core 2.0.17 (SAME as soundlight), so it builds today and a fresh resolve does NOT currently pull core 3.x. Hence LATENT, not live: downgraded critical->high. Still fix now — pinning is one line and removes the risk entirely.
findings.why_matters: Reproducibility of the deliverable is not guaranteed. It compiles today only because the resolved espressif32 is 7.0.1 (core 2.0.17). If/when the newest espressif32 in the registry ships Arduino core 3.x, a clean checkout (new laptop, cleared cache, fresh CI runner) would resolve to it and Esp32LedcPwm.cpp would fail to compile (undeclared ledcSetup/ledcAttachPin). All three servo/ESC outputs go through this class, so it would be a total build failure of the gift firmware, potentially days before the 21 Jul 2026 deadline.
findings.expected: control-fw pins espressif32 to the same known-good version soundlight uses (7.0.1), so any checkout reproducibly builds the same binary.
findings.suspected: Currently safe (resolves to 7.0.1); becomes a hard build failure whenever the unpinned resolution advances to a core-3.x platform.
findings.how_to_verify: Temporarily set platform = espressif32 to a core-3.x-providing version and observe the ledcSetup/ledcAttachPin compile errors; or `pio pkg show` the resolved core. Baseline: confirmed current resolve = 7.0.1 / core 2.0.17.
findings.fix_direction: Pin control-fw platform = espressif32 @ 7.0.1 (align with soundlight). If a future move to core 3.x is desired, migrate the LEDC HAL to ledcAttach/ledcWrite(pin,...) first, then pin to a 3.x version — never leave it unpinned.

findings.title: The matched board pair resolves toolchains independently (control unpinned vs soundlight ~7.0.1); latent divergence onto different Arduino cores
findings.evidence: control-fw platform unpinned; soundlight @ ~7.0.1. Neither repo commits a toolchain lock. Both target the identical MCU and must interoperate over link2 UART.
findings.lead_verification: Verified BOTH currently resolve to espressif32 @ 7.0.1 / core 2.0.17 — so TODAY they are on the same core, not divergent. Divergence is latent (control could float away from soundlight's pin on a future release). Downgraded high->medium.
findings.why_matters: The two firmwares are a matched pair talking over a byte protocol, yet resolve toolchains independently. Over time control could drift onto a newer core with different HardwareSerial/UART/timing while soundlight stays on 7.0.x, making any link2 mismatch far harder to debug and undermining 'it built for me' assurance.
findings.expected: Both repos pin espressif32 to the SAME explicit version, noted in each README.
findings.suspected: Latent: control-fw drifts to a newer core over time; soundlight stays 7.0.x.
findings.how_to_verify: Compare `pio pkg list` core versions after a clean cache (currently identical: 7.0.1).
findings.fix_direction: Pin both platformio.ini to the same espressif32 version (7.0.x — provides the legacy I2S driver soundlight needs AND the channel LEDC API control-fw uses).

findings.title: electron-builder never rebuilds serialport for the Electron ABI; the referenced 'app:rebuild' step does not exist — packaged .exe likely runs telemetry-disabled
findings.locator: electron-builder.yml lines 33-38 (comment: 'run via the app:rebuild step'); package.json scripts (no rebuild script); no afterPack/beforeBuild hook
findings.evidence: electron-builder.yml:34 comment references '@electron/rebuild, run via the app:rebuild step'. package.json scripts = setup/start/demo/test/test:watch/build/fetch-mediamtx — NO 'app:rebuild'. build = 'electron-builder --win' with no rebuild. @electron/rebuild ^3.6.0 is a devDependency but nothing invokes it; no beforeBuild/afterPack hook.
findings.lead_verification: CONFIRMED by lead (grep of package.json scripts + electron-builder.yml). The rebuild dependency exists but is dead-wired.
findings.why_matters: serialport ships a native .node compiled for a specific NODE_MODULE_VERSION. `npm run build` packages an unbuilt/system-ABI serialport. asarUnpack correctly unpacks the .node, but an ABI-mismatched binary throws at require(); CrsfSerialSource.js catches that and logs 'serialport unavailable ... telemetry disabled'. So the packaged app silently runs WITHOUT real battery/LQ/speed/gear telemetry — exactly the feature the .exe deploy path is meant to add. Green CI proves nothing (never packages).
findings.expected: package.json defines 'app:rebuild' (e.g. electron-rebuild -f -w serialport) and the build chains it (or a beforeBuild hook), so packaged serialport matches Electron 31's ABI.
findings.suspected: The .exe loads but serialport require() fails on ABI mismatch; telemetry silently stays simulated in the shipped app. Discovered only on-site with the car.
findings.how_to_verify: On Windows: npm run build, install, connect FT232, set W17_TELEMETRY_SOURCE=crsf-serial, check for 'CRSF serial open' vs 'serialport unavailable'. Or inspect the unpacked serialport .node NODE_MODULE_VERSION vs Electron 31.
findings.fix_direction: Add the 'app:rebuild' npm script using @electron/rebuild targeting serialport and invoke it before electron-builder (build script or beforeBuild hook); verify the unpacked .node ABI matches Electron 31.

findings.title: ground-station CI validates only pure logic — never packages the app nor rebuilds the native module, so green CI does not prove the shippable .exe works
findings.locator: whole file: checkout, setup-node@20, npm ci, npm test
findings.evidence: CI runs only `npm ci` + `npm test` (vitest). No `npm run build`, no @electron/rebuild, no Electron launch/smoke, no serialport load check.
findings.why_matters: 21/21 vitest covers shared/ pure logic (valuable) but gives false confidence about deployment. Every deploy-time failure (electron-builder regressions, serialport ABI, asarUnpack, mediamtx extraResources, the missing app:rebuild) sails past green CI. For a project whose deliverable is a Windows .exe, CI tests everything except the .exe.
findings.expected: A smoke-level packaging job (windows-latest, or electron-builder --dir, + serialport rebuild + optional headless boot).
findings.suspected: Packaging/native-module breakage discoverable only by manually running npm run build on Windows.
findings.how_to_verify: Add a windows-latest job running rebuild + electron-builder --dir; observe whether it currently fails on the serialport/app:rebuild gap.
findings.fix_direction: Add a packaging/smoke CI job (ideally windows-latest) that rebuilds serialport and runs electron-builder --dir; optionally a headless whenReady check.

findings.title: esp32dev_tuning env is never built in CI and can silently rot
findings.locator: lines 29-36: builds native, esp32dev, esp32dev_sim only
findings.evidence: CI builds esp32dev and esp32dev_sim but not esp32dev_tuning. Yet D8_BENCH_BRINGUP.md line 8 makes esp32dev_tuning THE firmware flashed for the entire bench bring-up, pulling in extra code behind -DW17_TUNING_CONSOLE (console lib, settings_hal_esp32 NVS/Preferences, Esp32SerialConsole) + #ifdef blocks in main.cpp not exercised by the other builds.
findings.why_matters: The tuning build compiles a distinct code path no automated build touches. A change to settings/console/main can break esp32dev_tuning while esp32dev + esp32dev_sim stay green. Since it's the build flashed for the whole hardware bring-up (steering trim, gear table, battery cal), a rotted tuning build blocks bench work on the critical path — undiscovered until first hardware use.
findings.expected: CI also runs `pio run -e esp32dev_tuning` (one-line addition; env already exists).
findings.suspected: esp32dev_tuning can accumulate compile breakage undetected.
findings.how_to_verify: Add the CI step, or run `pio run -e esp32dev_tuning` (baseline: it builds today).
findings.fix_direction: Add 'pio run -e esp32dev_tuning' to control-fw CI so all three ESP32 variants are gated.

findings.title: soundlight-fw lib_deps uses a caret range (^1.12.0) permitting any 1.x — not tightly pinned
findings.evidence: ^1.12.0 allows >=1.12.0 <2.0.0. The platform is carefully pinned to ~7.0.1 but the one third-party lib is not held to a single version.
findings.why_matters: Minor NeoPixel releases have changed ESP32 RMT/timing behavior; on a WS2812 strip sharing timing with I2S audio DMA (the sim doc flags 'watch for LED glitches while audio DMA runs') an unexpected bump could reintroduce tuned-out glitches. Low because the used API surface is stable, but it undercuts the repo's otherwise-deliberate pinning.
findings.suspected: A future checkout resolves NeoPixel to a newer 1.x than validated.
findings.how_to_verify: `pio pkg list` after clean resolve vs 1.12.0.
findings.fix_direction: Tighten the lib_deps constraint to exact/tilde.

findings.title: Flashing workflow underspecified: no port selection guidance, no control-fw README, easy to flash the wrong env onto the gift
findings.locator: docs/D8_BENCH_BRINGUP.md line 8; no README in repo root; platformio.ini has no upload_port/monitor_port
findings.evidence: control-fw has no README (only CLAUDE.md). Only flash instruction is D8 line 8: 'pio run -e esp32dev_tuning -t upload'. platformio.ini sets monitor_speed but no upload_port; no doc covers macOS /dev/tty.usbserial* vs Windows COMx, driver (CP210x/CH340), or upload_speed. Gift=esp32dev, bench=esp32dev_tuning, sim=esp32dev_sim; distinction relies on the -e flag.
findings.why_matters: The person flashing months later must know which of three envs, which port on which OS, correct driver. PlatformIO auto-detects the port usually, but multi-adapter benches (FT232 + flashing USB) can auto-pick wrong. Main hazard is env confusion: `-t upload` without `-e` uploads default esp32dev; flashing esp32dev_sim/_tuning as the final gift ships the self-feeder or an open UART0 console. D8 Phase 11 does say reflash plain esp32dev before gifting (mitigation).
findings.expected: A short control-fw README/flash section: the three envs and their purpose, per-OS upload command + port notes, and an explicit 'gift ships esp32dev only' warning.
findings.suspected: Correct env/port selection depends on operator memory; a wrong env flashes cleanly with no guard.
findings.how_to_verify: `pio run -t upload` (no -e) uploads esp32dev by default; no doc explains port selection per OS.
findings.fix_direction: Add a control-fw README/flash section enumerating envs, per-OS upload commands/port notes, a bold 'gift = esp32dev only' warning; consider explicit upload_speed.

findings.title: No Node version constraint (engines/.nvmrc); local Node 26 vs CI Node 20 vs Electron 31 bundled Node all differ
findings.evidence: package.json has no 'engines'; no .nvmrc/.node-version. Local Node v26, CI node 20, Electron 31 bundles ~Node 18. scripts/fetch-mediamtx.js relies on global fetch (Node 18+), unstated.
findings.why_matters: Three Node runtimes, no declared floor. A contributor on Node 16 breaks fetch-mediamtx (no global fetch) with an unhelpful error; @electron/rebuild cares about Node/Electron ABI pairing. Low because current versions work, but an undeclared assumption that hurts future setup.
findings.expected: An 'engines' field (node >=18) and/or committed .nvmrc; align CI node-version to it.
findings.how_to_verify: Run scripts/fetch-mediamtx.js under Node <18 -> 'fetch is not defined'.
findings.fix_direction: Add 'engines' + .nvmrc pinning the supported Node major; align CI.

findings.title: Inert 'allowScripts' key in package.json implies an install-gate policy no tool enforces
findings.evidence: package.json declares 'allowScripts' (the @lavamoat/allow-scripts config key), but there is no @lavamoat/allow-scripts dependency and no lavamoat in package-lock.json — nothing reads the key; npm ignores unknown keys. README/ensure-electron comments describe a 'lavamoat allowScripts gate'.
findings.why_matters: Documentation/reproducibility smell: implies script sandboxing the repo doesn't configure. A reader assumes scripts are gated (and ensure-electron is needed) when on a plain npm install they are not gated by this repo — behavior actually depends on the developer's global npm/corporate policy. The ensure-electron safety net is still correct to keep.
findings.expected: Either add + wire @lavamoat/allow-scripts (making the key meaningful) or drop the key and clarify the gate depends on the developer's environment.
findings.suspected: Dead config; actual behavior depends on the user's global npm policy.
findings.how_to_verify: `npm ls @lavamoat/allow-scripts` (absent); no tool references the key.
findings.fix_direction: Remove the inert key or add+wire the tool; align README wording.

findings.title: CI PlatformIO cache key hashes only platformio.ini, which never changes when an unpinned platform resolves to a new core
findings.locator: control-fw + soundlight-fw ci.yml line 24: key: pio-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('platformio.ini') }}
findings.evidence: actions/cache key hashes platformio.ini. For control-fw (no version pin) the key is stable across espressif32 releases. `pip install platformio` is also unpinned.
findings.why_matters: Compounds the unpinned-platform finding: a cache hit keeps CI building against a stale-but-working cached core, masking that a fresh environment would resolve to a newer, incompatible core. CI green is not evidence a fresh checkout builds. Low alone; meaningful only with the pinning finding.
findings.expected: With platform pinned, the cache key is honest; optionally pin PlatformIO in the pip step.
findings.suspected: CI may keep passing on a cached 2.x core even after upstream would resolve to 3.x for a fresh checkout.
findings.how_to_verify: Clear the Actions cache / bump the key and re-run CI.
findings.fix_direction: Pin the platform (primary), consider pinning PlatformIO version, optionally include resolved core version in the cache key.

open_questions: When (if ever) will the PlatformIO 'espressif32' platform's newest release ship Arduino core 3.x? That timing determines when the unpinned control-fw goes from latent to a live build failure. (Today it resolves to 7.0.1 / core 2.0.17.)
Does `npm ci` on CI build serialport's native binary (optionalDependency) against Node 20 at all, and is it ever exercised by npm test — or is serialport entirely absent from the tested path?
Is there any committed record of the exact espressif32 version the running hardware was validated against, so a future rebuild reproduces the gifted binary?
On core 2.0.17, do analogSetPinAttenuation + analogReadMilliVolts (the eFuse-cal ADC path) behave as the battery code assumes? Revalidate if ever moved to a 3.x pin.

hw_validation_items.item: Pin control-fw espressif32 to 7.0.1, then do a clean-cache `pio run -e esp32dev` on a fresh machine to prove the gift firmware reproducibly builds (not just from a warm cache).
Confirm both boards are flashed from the SAME pinned espressif32 version and record it in a build log.
On Windows, run full `npm run build` WITH a working serialport rebuild, install the .exe, and confirm CrsfSerialSource logs 'CRSF serial open' (not 'serialport unavailable') with the FT232 connected.
Verify upload-port selection on the real bench (FT232 for CRSF + ESP32 flashing USB both connected); document the port per OS.
Verify soundlight I2S produces clean audio through the legacy IDF driver on the pinned core — confirms ~7.0.1 delivers the legacy driver semantics.
Before gifting, confirm the control board is flashed with plain esp32dev (not sim/tuning) — D8 Phase 11.
